pairs.definition: A record of all shareholders and their ownership stakes
A plan defining when equity gradually becomes owned
Reduction in ownership percentage from new shares being issued
Shares with special rights ranking above common in exits
Standard equity typically held by founders and employees
All shares including unexercised options and convertibles
Right to invest in future rounds to maintain ownership
Right to match a purchase offer before shares go to a third party
Right to sell shares on the same terms as a major seller
Right to force minority shareholders into a company sale
Entitlement to receive regular financial and operational reports
Attends board meetings but cannot vote
Simple Agreement for Future Equity with no interest or maturity
Short-term debt that converts to equity at the next round
Maximum valuation used to calculate conversion price
Percentage reduction on share price at conversion
Deadline by which debt must be repaid or converted
Short-term financing between funding rounds
Company value before new investment is added
Company value after new investment is added
Funding at a lower valuation than the previous round
Valuation calculated as a factor of annual revenue
Annualized value of recurring subscription revenue
Valuation based on projected future cash flows discounted to present
Right to receive a specific amount before common in an exit
Gets preference amount plus pro-rata share of remainder
Acquisition primarily to hire the target company's talent
Portion of price paid contingent on future performance
Third-party holding account for potential post-close claims
Shows sequential order of shareholder payouts in an exit
Investor providing capital with no management role
Fund manager making investment decisions
GP's share of profits, typically 20% above the hurdle
Annual fee paid by LPs to cover fund operations
Request from GP to LPs to transfer committed capital
Pattern of negative early returns turning positive over time
Employee Stock Ownership Plan for equity compensation
Virtual shares paying cash equivalent without real equity
Predetermined price at which options can be exercised
Minimum period before any equity vests
Favorable treatment of departing employee's equity
Forfeiture of equity when leaving under unfavorable terms
Reprices all investor shares to match the new lower price
Includes all shares in calculation for a smaller adjustment
Uses only preferred shares for a larger adjustment
Must invest in future rounds or lose preferred rights
First opportunity to buy newly issued shares
Veto power over certain major company decisions
Non-binding document outlining key investment terms
Comprehensive investigation before making an investment
Contract requiring parties to keep information confidential
Transferring intellectual property ownership to the company
Allows withdrawal if significant negative changes occur
Requirement that must be fulfilled before a deal closes
Right to appoint a representative to the board
Action requiring specific approval beyond simple majority
Obligation to act in the best interest of represented parties
Minimum members required for a valid meeting
Approval threshold higher than 50%, often two-thirds
Liability coverage for directors and officers

true_false_statements.statement: A ROFR gives existing investors the right to participate in future funding rounds.
true_false_statements.explanation: ROFR is about matching third-party offers on share transfers. Pro-rata rights are for participating in new rounds.

true_false_statements.statement: A SAFE is a type of debt instrument with an interest rate.
true_false_statements.explanation: A SAFE is NOT debt. It has no interest rate, no maturity date, and no repayment obligation.

true_false_statements.statement: Full ratchet anti-dilution is the most founder-friendly form of anti-dilution protection.
true_false_statements.explanation: Full ratchet is the MOST investor-friendly. Broad-based weighted average is the most founder-friendly.

true_false_statements.statement: A liquidation preference determines the order of payouts to shareholders in an exit.
true_false_statements.explanation: Liquidation preferences set who gets paid first and how much before common shareholders receive anything.

true_false_statements.statement: Post-money valuation equals pre-money valuation plus the new investment amount.
true_false_statements.explanation: Post-money = pre-money + investment. If pre-money is €8M and investment is €2M, post-money is €10M.

true_false_statements.statement: Common shareholders typically receive their money back before preferred shareholders in a liquidation.
true_false_statements.explanation: Preferred shareholders receive their liquidation preference BEFORE common shareholders get anything.

true_false_statements.statement: A 4-year vesting schedule with a 1-year cliff means 25% vests after the first year.
true_false_statements.explanation: At the 1-year cliff, 25% (one quarter) vests. The remaining 75% vests monthly over the next 3 years.

true_false_statements.statement: Drag-along rights protect minority shareholders from being forced to sell.
true_false_statements.explanation: Drag-along rights allow MAJORITY holders to FORCE minority shareholders to participate in a sale.

true_false_statements.statement: Tag-along rights allow minority shareholders to join in when a major shareholder sells.
true_false_statements.explanation: Tag-along (co-sale) rights let minority investors sell alongside a major shareholder on the same terms.

true_false_statements.statement: A term sheet is a legally binding contract.
true_false_statements.explanation: Most term sheet provisions are non-binding. Only specific clauses like confidentiality are typically binding.

true_false_statements.statement: The general partner in a VC fund provides the majority of the capital.
true_false_statements.explanation: Limited partners provide the capital (typically 99%). The GP manages the fund and typically contributes 1-2%.

true_false_statements.statement: Carried interest is typically 20% of the fund's profits.
true_false_statements.explanation: Standard carry is 20% of profits above the hurdle rate, serving as the GP's performance-based compensation.

true_false_statements.statement: A down round always means the company is failing.
true_false_statements.explanation: While a down round signals challenges, it can also reflect broader market corrections. Companies can recover from down rounds.

true_false_statements.statement: PRO-rata rights allow investors to increase their ownership percentage in future rounds.
true_false_statements.explanation: Pro-rata rights allow investors to MAINTAIN their percentage, not increase it. Super pro-rata rights allow increases.

true_false_statements.statement: In a participating preferred structure, investors get both their preference AND a share of remaining proceeds.
true_false_statements.explanation: Participating preferred (double-dip) means investors get their preference back PLUS share in the remainder pro-rata.

true_false_statements.statement: A VSOP gives employees actual shares in the company.
true_false_statements.explanation: VSOPs provide virtual shares with cash-equivalent payouts. They do NOT grant actual equity or voting rights.

true_false_statements.statement: The J-curve in venture capital shows that fund returns are typically negative in early years.
true_false_statements.explanation: Early fund years show negative returns as fees are paid and investments haven't matured. Returns improve as exits occur.

true_false_statements.statement: An IPO is the only way for VC investors to exit their investment.
true_false_statements.explanation: Investors can exit through acquisitions, secondary sales, buybacks, or redemption rights in addition to IPOs.

true_false_statements.statement: A valuation cap on a convertible note sets the MINIMUM conversion valuation.
true_false_statements.explanation: A valuation cap sets the MAXIMUM effective valuation for conversion, protecting the investor from too-high valuations.

true_false_statements.statement: Broad-based weighted average anti-dilution includes all outstanding shares in its calculation.
true_false_statements.explanation: Broad-based includes common, preferred, options, and warrants in the denominator, resulting in a smaller adjustment.

true_false_statements.statement: A no-shop clause prevents the company from talking to other potential investors.
true_false_statements.explanation: A no-shop clause prevents seeking alternative offers during a specified period after signing a term sheet.

true_false_statements.statement: Runway equals total funding raised divided by monthly revenue.
true_false_statements.explanation: Runway = cash on hand / monthly burn rate. It measures months until cash runs out, not a revenue ratio.

true_false_statements.statement: Preferred shares always convert to common shares in an IPO.
true_false_statements.explanation: In a typical IPO, all preferred shares convert to common shares, as public companies generally have a single share class for public trading.

true_false_statements.statement: A board observer has the same voting rights as a board member.
true_false_statements.explanation: A board observer can attend meetings and access materials but CANNOT vote on board resolutions.

true_false_statements.statement: The option pool shuffle benefits new investors at the expense of existing shareholders.
true_false_statements.explanation: Including the option pool in pre-money means existing shareholders bear all the dilution, not the new investor.

true_false_statements.statement: DPI measures total fund performance including unrealized investments.
true_false_statements.explanation: DPI (Distributions to Paid-In) measures only ACTUAL CASH returned. TVPI includes both realized and unrealized value.

true_false_statements.statement: An earn-out guarantees the seller will receive the full acquisition price.
true_false_statements.explanation: An earn-out makes part of the price CONTINGENT on future performance. If targets are missed, the seller receives less.

true_false_statements.statement: Anti-dilution provisions protect all shareholders equally.
true_false_statements.explanation: Anti-dilution typically protects only preferred shareholders. Common shareholders (founders, employees) usually have no such protection.

true_false_statements.statement: A flat round means the company raised at the same valuation as the previous round.
true_false_statements.explanation: A flat round indicates no valuation increase since the last round, suggesting stagnant growth.

true_false_statements.statement: Redemption rights give the company the ability to force investors to sell back their shares.
true_false_statements.explanation: Redemption rights give INVESTORS the right to force the COMPANY to repurchase their shares, not the other way around.
